title: Ear Infection / Otitis Media
summary: Infection of the middle or outer ear. Extremely common in children under 5. Causes significant pain and can affect hearing. Most resolve with or without antibiotics but need assessment.
steps: Identify the location. Middle ear infection (otitis media) — pain felt deep inside the ear, often following a cold, with muffled hearing and sometimes fever. Outer ear infection (otitis externa, swimmer's ear) — pain in the ear canal, worsens when you pull the outer ear, often follows swimming or moisture in the ear.
For pain relief: paracetamol or ibuprofen taken regularly rather than waiting for pain to become severe.
Warm compress against the ear — a warm cloth or warm water bottle wrapped in cloth — provides significant pain relief.
Keep the ear dry. Do not allow water to enter the ear canal during bathing — cover with a cotton wool ball lightly coated in petroleum jelly.
Do not insert anything into the ear canal — no cotton buds, pins, or fingers. This pushes infection deeper and damages the delicate canal.
Most middle ear infections in children over 2 years resolve without antibiotics within 2 to 3 days. Pain management and monitoring is appropriate for mild cases.
Children under 2 with ear infection should see a doctor — they are at higher risk of complications.
If discharge is coming from the ear — the eardrum may have perforated. This is actually a sign the pressure has relieved and the pain usually improves. Keep the ear dry and see a doctor.
warnings: Never insert anything into the ear canal.
Do not use ear drops unless prescribed — some drops are harmful if the eardrum has perforated.
Recurrent ear infections in children cause cumulative hearing loss — get medical assessment.
Do not ignore ear infection in an infant — they cannot describe the pain and may just be unusually irritable.
redFlags: Swelling, redness, and tenderness behind the ear — mastoiditis, serious complication.
Facial weakness on the same side as the ear infection.
Severe dizziness or balance problems with ear pain.
Discharge from the ear in a child under 2.
High fever above 39°C with ear pain.
No improvement after 48 to 72 hours of pain management.

helpful: Paracetamol or ibuprofen for pain — ibuprofen is particularly effective for ear pain
Olive oil drops — 2 to 3 drops of warm olive oil in the ear canal for pain relief in otitis externa
avoid: Cotton buds in the ear canal
Swimming or allowing water in the ear during infection
Prescribed ear drops if the eardrum is perforated — check with a doctor first
askFor: Amoxicillin if antibiotic is needed — standard first-line for middle ear infection
Otomize or Sofradex ear drops for outer ear infection only — not if eardrum perforated
Audiometry referral if recurrent infections affecting hearing

whenToGetHelp: See a doctor for ear infection in a child under 2, any ear infection with fever above 39°C, swelling behind the ear, facial weakness, or no improvement after 3 days of pain management. All ear infections with discharge should be assessed.
